title: FiveThirtyEight：拜登獲勝機率為創紀錄的87.6%

replies.msg:
Leonid Bershidsky
2020年10月19日週一 下午10:06 [GMT+8]·1 分鐘文章
【彭博】-- 根據民意調查分析機構FiveThirtyEight的選舉預測模型，喬·拜登贏得多數選舉人票的機率從10月18日的87.4%升至創紀錄的87.6%，預計他將贏得538張選舉人票中的350張。

該模型估計唐納德·川普的獲勝機率為11.9%，低於10月18日的12.2%根據10月19日的模型，川普贏得普選票的概率為4%，拜登是96%川普的全國民調平均支持率在10月19日達到41.8%，10月18日為41.9%拜登的全國民調平均支持率在10月19日達到52.4%，10月18日為52.3%

Notice: Don’t count the underdog out! Upset wins are surprising but not impossible.

replies.msg:
The polls and computer analysis have very good records for decades for the US elections.

The polls predicted correctly for the popular votes for the 2016 election, but failed for the electoral votes. The failures are due to two reasons:
(1) The pollsters have NOT made any new polls in the last few days before the election, but there was a late swing to support Trump in these few days.
(2) There were a voting difference between white people who had go to college and those who had not. These difference has never existed in precious election.

In order to correct the second problem, the polls have taken into account the white people who had or had not go to college this year. So the polls should be more accurate this year.

For the first problem. the pollsters MAY make polls even two ot three days before the election.

quote.msg: 低過希拉里，大撚鑊

replies.msg:
The New York Times is just one poll.
The average of the polls gave Clinton a much smaller lead.
You can see all the high quality polls at here:
https://www.realclearpolitics.com/epolls/2020/president/us/general_election_trump_vs_biden-6247.html

The average of all high quality pool is much better than any single poll because the total sample size is much larger than that of any single poll.

Furthermore, the method used by FiveThirtyEight is different: It does based on all the major poll data. But it includes the effect of early voting this year because all people (including Trump) believe that more early voting will be good for Biden. Then it runs a computer program to made 40,000 elections. The prediction of FiveThirtyEight is based on this 40,000 elections.

So, Trump wins about 4,800 elections out of these 40,000 elections and Biden wins about 37,200 times.

Remember: the prediction is correct no matter which one actually win the election.

replies.msg:
You have to read the full poll resuilt because when one do not want to answer, he/she usually just do not answer one of a few question(s) . Usually almost all of them will answer some questions.

For the question on Presidential Vote Intent 2020, some of the polls will put these people as "No sure" For example, you can jump to page 39 of this document to read the poll data on this question:
https://docs.cdn.yougov.com/1ace2lgidt/econTabReport.pdf


Usually, more young men refuse to answer, while women and old people are more likely to answer.

New voting data:

With early voting well underway across the country, the percentage of ballots already cast is growing rapidly — and Biden is banking far more votes than Trump. Last week, 17 percent of likely voters said they had already voted; this week, that percentage has more than doubled, to 38 percent. Given the inherent lag of mail balloting and recent nature of most of these votes — 75 percent of early voters say they voted during the past week — such numbers suggest that the early vote tally could soon hit an unprecedented 60 million.

That's the best knowledge we (human) have. Probability is also the true aspect of Nature.

Probability is not empty words. I can tell you an example: the first atomic clock (atomic clocks are the most accurate clocks we had) is based on probability!

This is the limit God/Nature allowed us to predict the future.
Take this as example: if you throw a fair die, will you spend 1 dollar to bet on an even number (which has 1/2 chance to win) or on 'number 1' (which has 1/6 chance to win)? If you win, you always only get 2 dollars.

replies.msg:
Actually, the prediction in the 2016 is not incorrect. Mathematically, the prediction is correct.

It is our psychological mind (rather mathematical knowledge) to say the prediction is wrong: We (human) have got used to certainty for millions of years and we are still very uncomfortable with probability. Even Albert Einstein who has contributed so much to quantum physics opposes quantum mechanics because of the probabilistic nature of quantum mechanics. Despite all the victories of quantum theory, we only know modern quantum theory for 94 years, we still need at leat hundreds of years to change our psychological mind.

If you asked the mathematicians, they will all conclude that the prediction for 2016 election is consistent with the actual result so the prediction is not incorrect.

replies.msg:
民調 is even more probabilistic than physical world because 民調 subjects to even more influence factors and is much more complicated.

Furthermore, 民調 is also affected by physical world. For example, Trump hope that large number of his supporters will come out to vote on 3 Nov., and the danger is that 3 Nov may have a very bad weather so that much fewer people will come out to vote. Hence, Trump is also now asking his support to vote earlier. Weather is chaotic (one kind of probability).

The election will be affected by a lot of physical, biological and psychological laws, hence, it is even more probabilistic than a physical system. Human as a biological objects consists of many molecules and each of these molecules are governed by the probablistic quantum mechanics.

I welcome you to point out any concrete mistakes in my post.

quote.msg:
The New York Times is just one poll.
The average of the polls gave Clinton a much smaller lead.
You can see all the high quality polls at here:
https://www.realclearpolitics.com/epolls/2020/president/us/general_election_trump_vs_biden-6247.html

The average of all high quality pool is much better than any single poll because the total sample size is much larger than that of any single poll.

Furthermore, the method used by FiveThirtyEight is different: It does based on all the major poll data. But it includes the effect of early voting this year because all people (including Trump) believe that more early voting will be good for Biden. Then it runs a computer program to made 40,000 elections. The prediction of FiveThirtyEight is based on this 40,000 elections.

So, Trump wins about 4,800 elections out of these 40,000 elections and Biden wins about 37,200 times.

Remember: the prediction is correct no matter which one actually win the election.

replies.msg:
We do NOT know whether high quality 民調 polls have problems. Three things need to be considered when we try to determine whether a poll is good:

(1) A hidden assumption behind all polls.
(2) There are statistical errors in the polls. These are relatively easy to take into account and this is usually included as part of the polling error.
(3) There MAY be systematic errors in the polls. These are very hard to handle and they may or may not be included as part of the polling error.

I will discuss these issues after my dinner.

We do NOT know whether high quality 民調 polls have problems. Three things need to be considered when we try to determine whether a poll is good:

(1) A hidden assumption behind all polls.
(2) There are statistical errors in the polls. These are relatively easy to take into account and this is usually included as part of the polling error.
(3) There MAY be systematic errors in the polls. These are very hard to handle and they may or may not be included as part of the polling error.

I will discuss these issues after my dinner.

replies.msg:
(1) A hidden assumption behind all polls.

The hidden assumption behind all polls is that all the people will answer the question honestly. This assumption is almost certainly wrong. However, we must make this assumption; otherwise all polls will be rejected. We want to know ahead something about the election before the election day, so we have to conduct polls and hence use this assumption.

This does not mean I will NEVER question the correctness of this assumption. A few years ago, a friend asked me this question, "The Labor Party or the Conservative Party will win the British election? The polls suggested that this would be a very tight election."

My answer was "The British do not answer the questions honestly in the Exit Poll, so the British opinion polls might NOT be indicative." If you want me to use my money bet on that British election, I will follow the results of those poll because that is the best knowledge we have.(even though I question their correctness). The election result is that the Conservative Party win the election in landslide (So, the result actually confirmed what I said about the British opinion polls).

In USA, people has answered the Exit Poll so honestly that the TV stations often declared the election result of a state long before the officials have finished counting the votes.
Furthermore, in previous US elections, the opinion polls have been quite accurate. The opinion polls are quite accurate even in predicting the popular votes of the 2016 election. Although some people said that the polls were wrong in predicting the electoral votes (I will discuss this issue when I discusse the systematic errors later).

We do NOT know whether high quality 民調 polls have problems. Three things need to be considered when we try to determine whether a poll is good:

(1) A hidden assumption behind all polls.
(2) There are statistical errors in the polls. These are relatively easy to take into account and this is usually included as part of the polling error.
(3) There MAY be systematic errors in the polls. These are very hard to handle and they may or may not be included as part of the polling error.

I will discuss these issues after my dinner.

(1) A hidden assumption behind all polls.

The hidden assumption behind all polls is that all the people will answer the question honestly. This assumption is almost certainly wrong. However, we must make this assumption; otherwise all polls will be rejected. We want to know ahead something about the election before the election day, so we have to conduct polls and hence use this assumption.

This does not mean I will NEVER question the correctness of this assumption. A few years ago, a friend asked me this question, "The Labor Party or the Conservative Party will win the British election? The polls suggested that this would be a very tight election."

My answer was "The British do not answer the questions honestly in the Exit Poll, so the British opinion polls might NOT be indicative." If you want me to use my money bet on that British election, I will follow the results of those poll because that is the best knowledge we have.(even though I question their correctness). The election result is that the Conservative Party win the election in landslide (So, the result actually confirmed what I said about the British opinion polls).

In USA, people has answered the Exit Poll so honestly that the TV stations often declared the election result of a state long before the officials have finished counting the votes.
Furthermore, in previous US elections, the opinion polls have been quite accurate. The opinion polls are quite accurate even in predicting the popular votes of the 2016 election. Although some people said that the polls were wrong in predicting the electoral votes (I will discuss this issue when I discusse the systematic errors later).

replies.msg: (2) There are standard mathematical methods to handle those statistical errors. So unless there are some careless mistakes in the calculations, all honest polls should be quite reliable. We only need to remember the quoted poll errors when we interpret the result of those polls.

quote.msg:
We do NOT know whether high quality 民調 polls have problems. Three things need to be considered when we try to determine whether a poll is good:

(1) A hidden assumption behind all polls.
(2) There are statistical errors in the polls. These are relatively easy to take into account and this is usually included as part of the polling error.
(3) There MAY be systematic errors in the polls. These are very hard to handle and they may or may not be included as part of the polling error.

I will discuss these issues after my dinner.

(1) A hidden assumption behind all polls.

The hidden assumption behind all polls is that all the people will answer the question honestly. This assumption is almost certainly wrong. However, we must make this assumption; otherwise all polls will be rejected. We want to know ahead something about the election before the election day, so we have to conduct polls and hence use this assumption.

This does not mean I will NEVER question the correctness of this assumption. A few years ago, a friend asked me this question, "The Labor Party or the Conservative Party will win the British election? The polls suggested that this would be a very tight election."

My answer was "The British do not answer the questions honestly in the Exit Poll, so the British opinion polls might NOT be indicative." If you want me to use my money bet on that British election, I will follow the results of those poll because that is the best knowledge we have.(even though I question their correctness). The election result is that the Conservative Party win the election in landslide (So, the result actually confirmed what I said about the British opinion polls).

In USA, people has answered the Exit Poll so honestly that the TV stations often declared the election result of a state long before the officials have finished counting the votes.
Furthermore, in previous US elections, the opinion polls have been quite accurate. The opinion polls are quite accurate even in predicting the popular votes of the 2016 election. Although some people said that the polls were wrong in predicting the electoral votes (I will discuss this issue when I discusse the systematic errors later).

(2) There are standard mathematical methods to handle those statistical errors. So unless there are some careless mistakes in the calculations, all honest polls should be quite reliable. We only need to remember the quoted poll errors when we interpret the result of those polls.

replies.msg:
(3) Systematic errors are very hard to handle and the pollsters will try to estimate the size of the systematic errors and include these errors into the total poll errors. However, in many cases, we have the systematic errors just because we do not know there are such errors. Hence, in these cases, we have the systematic errors just because of the limit of our knowledge. We should not conclude that the polls have problems because that is beyond our knowledge. The pollsters have tried their best to conduct the polls. An example of this situation is the 2016 election. After the election, people found that the white people who have been in colleges and the while people who have NOT been in colleges had different voting patterns. Since these different voting patterns did not exist in previous elections, all the polls conducted for the 2016 election have not considered this issue and hence they all have a systematic error. If this systematic error has been included, then the predication of the polls is LIKELY to be better. However, even with this systematic error, the predication is not too bad: Trump has a REASONABLY chance to win the election. Since Clinton lost the election, her supporters blame the polls. However, these supporters are driving by their emotions and mis-interpret the poll results (even though a lot of them have enough mathematical knowledge to understand the true meaning of polls.)
This year, all the polls have included the effects of these different polling patterns. So up the now, with the best of our knowledge, these polls do not have any known problems and they represent the best we can do right now.

Biden winnn

New in the polls Tuesday: Race narrows in North Carolina, Florida; voters nationally favor Biden over Trump



A University of North Florida poll narrowly puts Biden up one percentage point over Trump, well within the margin of error.
Trump rose five percent from earlier this month in the same poll. A key base of his — senior voters — is showing up in smaller numbers than in 2016 exit polls. Biden, meanwhile, is struggling Hispanic voters, a key Democratic voting bloc in the previous election.

A poll conducted by Washington Post/ABC News also effectively puts Trump and Biden in range of one another, with Biden only up by one percent.
A small majority of registered voters (51%) in the state disapprove of Trump's presidency, but that number remains slightly lower than national ratings.

Biden up 9% nationally: New York Times


A poll by the New York Times and Siena College finds Biden ahead of Trump, overall, with a margin of 9 points (50 to 41 percent).

With a surge of support from women, people of color, white people with college degrees — as well as white women and older voters, which leaned toward Trump in 2016 — these polls paint Biden in a comfortable position. Trump, however, still maintains a stronghold over white people without college degrees and men.

The poll also found a majority of Americans supported mask wearing (59%), a comprehensive stimulus package (72%), a $2 trillion renewable energy plan (66%) and even a public option for health insurance (66%). However, Americans remain doubtful of vaccines — as a third would likely or definitely not take a vaccine even with FDA approval — and court packing (58%).

On 10/21/2020 01:09 AM Eastern Time

The following statements represent the general opinions of the polling industry on the election 2016.:

If you ask Americans whether they trust the polls, many seem unable to let go of what happened in 2016. Polls taken since then have generally found that a majority of Americans have at least some doubts about what polls say. But as FiveThirtyEight wrote in the run-up to the 2016 election, Donald Trump was always a normal polling error behind Hillary Clinton.

And that’s essentially what happened in 2016: Trump beat his polls by just a few points in just a few states. The presidential polls were, simply, not that off. State-level polling was less accurate, although as editor-in-chief Nate Silver wrote after the election, it was “still within the ‘normal’ range of accuracy.”

replies.msg:
So, the prediction of the average of the polls for 2016 election is actually bad and the polls should NOT be considered as a failure.

The failure is NOT in the polls, but it is in the mind of people who have mis-interpret the prediction. For those still remember the statistics they learnt at college: there are two main characteristics to describe a statistical distribution: the mean and the standard deviation. For the current purpose, the standard deviation is roughly the polling error. If one do not consider the polling error (the standard deviation), then he/she will mis-interpret the predication of the average of the polls.
